Here’s a cleaned-up, **complete replacement** for your `AMiOffice.vbproj` that will:

- keep your current settings,
- generate a **stable product version** (e.g. `1.0`) that you control in one place,
- generate an **auto build number that changes every build** (timestamp-based, always increasing),
- generate `BuildInfo.g.vb` every build and compile it,
- avoid duplicate-compile problems.

It also keeps your existing `BuildTimestamp` idea, but adds a **BuildNumber** and a combined **DisplayVersion** string you can use in both the About dialog and window title.

### Replace your entire `AMiOffice.vbproj` with this

```xml name=AMiOffice.vbproj
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>
    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <!--
      Manual product version (what you want to show as "Ver 1.0").
      Change ONLY this when you want a new major/minor.
    -->
    <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>

    <!-- Keep assembly/file versions stable unless you want to manage them too -->
    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>

    <!-- Keep NuGet/package/app version stable too; we'll show build separately -->
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <!-- Explicit path so we don't depend on IntermediateOutputPath -->
    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

    <Copyright>Copyright Kirk Saffell 2026</Copyright>
    <Title>AMiOfficeApplication</Title>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System.Data" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Settings.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTimeSharedInput>True</DesignTimeSharedInput>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Settings.settings</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
    <None Update="My Project\Settings.settings">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>SettingsSingleFileGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Settings.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- IMPORTANT: do NOT compile a stray root BuildInfo.g.vb -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Prevent any default globs from also including the generated file (avoids duplicate Compile items) -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb and add it to compilation -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <!-- UTC timestamp shown in About / title if you want -->
      <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>

      <!-- Auto build number (increasing, compact). Example: 20260417.1530 -->
      <BuildNumber>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyyMMdd.HHmm"))</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = "$(ProductVersion)"
    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = "$(BuildNumber)"
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = "$(BuildTimestamp)"

    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String
        Get
            Return "Ver " & ProductVersion & " Build " & BuildNumber
        End Get
    End Property
End Module
" />

    <!-- Add AFTER generation so it can't be missing -->
    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

## How to use it in your title/About
Use the combined string:

- `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`

Example:

```vbnet
Me.Text = "AMiOffice - " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

And in About dialog label:
```vbnet
lblVersion.Text = BuildInfo.DisplayVersion & " (" & BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp & ")"
```

## Note about “0.1, 0.2…” specifically
The above uses a **timestamp build number** (e.g. `20260417.1530`) which always increases and needs no storage. If you truly want **0.1 / 0.2 / 0.3** as a counter, we can do it, but it requires storing/incrementing a counter file (or using Git commit count / CI run number).

If you confirm “timestamp build number is fine”, you’re done after replacing the `.vbproj` and switching your UI to `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`.

Your UI code is still referencing `BuildInfo.VersionText`, but in the replacement `.vbproj` I generated `BuildInfo` with:

- `DisplayVersion`

### Fix option 1 (smallest change): add `VersionText` back into the generated BuildInfo
Edit your `AMiOffice.vbproj` target and replace the `WriteLinesToFile` `Lines=` content with this version (adds `VersionText` and keeps everything else):

```xml
<WriteLinesToFile
  File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
  Overwrite="true"
  Lines="Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = "$(ProductVersion)"
    Friend Const VersionText As String = "$(ProductVersion)"
    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = "$(BuildNumber)"
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = "$(BuildTimestamp)"

    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String
        Get
            Return "Ver " & VersionText & " Build " & BuildNumber
        End Get
    End Property
End Module
" />
```

Now your existing references to `BuildInfo.VersionText` will compile again.

Wherever you have:


(And keep using `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp` if you want the time shown separately.)

You have 3 practical ways to get `Build 42`, in order of simplicity:

---

### Quick question (determines the best solution)
Do you want the number to increment when you:
1) press **Build** in Visual Studio, **every time**, or
2) run your `dotnet publish ...` (your `amoffpub`) **only when publishing**?

If you answer that, I’ll give you the exact minimal edits to your `.vbproj` to display exactly:

`AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 42)`

(without introducing annoying side effects).
only when I publish

Perfect—incrementing **only on publish** is the least annoying (no constant git diffs during normal builds).

Below is a simple, reliable approach that requires **one small file** in your project plus a small change to your `.vbproj`.

## 1) Create a counter file (once)
Create this file in your project root (same folder as `AMiOffice.vbproj`):

```text name=build-counter.txt
0
```

Commit it (so it persists across machines).

## 2) Update your `.vbproj` (replace your current GenerateBuildInfo target)
In `AMiOffice.vbproj`, replace the entire `<Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" ...>` with this **publish-only** pair of targets.

This does two things:
- on **Publish**: increments `build-counter.txt`
- on **Build and Publish**: generates `BuildInfo.g.vb` (so builds still compile), but the number only increments on publish

```xml name=AMiOffice.vbproj
  <!-- Increment build number ONLY when publishing -->
  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Ensure the file exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- Read current number -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Write incremented number back -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo for builds and publishes -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

      <!-- Your manual product version -->
      <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>

      <!-- If BuildNumber wasn't set by Publish (normal Build), read current file without increment -->
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
      <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss 'UTC'"))</BuildTimestamp>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Make sure counter exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- If BuildNumber is empty (normal build), read current counter -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''" File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''">
      <BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = "$(ProductVersion)"
    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = "$(BuildNumber)"
    Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = "$(BuildTimestamp)"

    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String
        Get
            Return "AMiOffice v" & ProductVersion & " (Build " & BuildNumber & ")"
        End Get
    End Property
End Module
" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>
```

### What you’ll see
- Normal **Build**: build number stays whatever is in `build-counter.txt`
- **Publish**: increments (0 → 1 → 2 → 3 …) and the published EXE will show the new number

## 3) Use it in your window title/About
Set title to:

```vbnet
Me.Text = BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

(or prefix with your longer app name if you want)

## Important note
Every time you publish, `build-counter.txt` will change—so you’ll want to **commit that change** (or at least be aware it will show as a modified file in git).

If you want, tell me whether you’re using git and whether you publish from VS or `dotnet publish`, and I’ll tailor the target to avoid any “file is read-only” / permissions / line-ending gotchas.
Commit it (so it persists across machines). what does this mean?

“Commit it” means: **add that file to your Git repository and make a Git commit**, so the file (and its current build number) is saved in source control and will come along when you copy/cloned the repo to another computer.

If you *don’t* commit it, then:

- it will exist only on the computer where you created it, and
- another computer (or a fresh clone) won’t have the counter, so the build number would reset or be missing.

### What you do (typical Git workflow)
From your repo folder:

```bat
git status
git add build-counter.txt
git commit -m "Add publish build counter"
```

After that, if you work on another machine and do:

```bat
git clone ...
```

…it will include `build-counter.txt` and your build number will continue from the same place.

### If you’re *not* using Git
Then “commit it” doesn’t apply. In that case you’d need to manually copy `build-counter.txt` along with your project folder whenever you move the project to a new machine.

If you tell me whether you’re using GitHub / git for this project (and whether you ever build on more than one PC), I can recommend the best build-number approach (counter file vs timestamp vs CI run number).
not using git

Then you don’t need to “commit” anything—there’s no source control to save it into.

What it means for the build counter approach:

- The file `build-counter.txt` will live in your project folder.
- Each time you **Publish**, MSBuild will update that file (0 → 1 → 2 → 3…).
- As long as you keep building/publishing from the **same project folder on the same PC**, the number will keep increasing.

### If you move the project to another computer
You must copy `build-counter.txt` along with the project, or the counter will reset.

Two easy ways to handle that:

1) **Copy the whole project folder** (recommended), ensuring `build-counter.txt` goes with it.
2) If you already moved the project and forgot the file, just create `build-counter.txt` again and put the last number you used inside (e.g., `42`).

### Alternative (no file to carry around)
If you don’t want any counter file at all, use the timestamp build number instead. It always increases and works on any machine without keeping state, but it looks like:
- `AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 20260417.1530)`

If you tell me whether you ever plan to build/publish on more than one PC, I’ll tell you which option will be least hassle.
only publishing on one pc, prefer to use build and not time stamp

Then the **publish-only counter file** approach is exactly what you want, and it’s not much work:

- add `build-counter.txt` once
- MSBuild increments it **only when you publish**
- your title/About shows `AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 42)` (no timestamp required)

## 1) Create the counter file
In the same folder as `AMiOffice.vbproj`, create:

```text name=build-counter.txt
0
```

## 2) Update your `AMiOffice.vbproj`
You already have a `GenerateBuildInfo` target. Replace your current target with the following **two targets** (one increments on Publish; one generates BuildInfo for compilation):

> Paste these just before `</Project>` and remove your old `GenerateBuildInfo` target to avoid duplicates.

```xml name=AMiOffice.vbproj
  <!-- Increment build number ONLY when publishing -->
  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Ensure the file exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- Read current number -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <!-- Increment -->
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Write back -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <!-- Generate BuildInfo.g.vb for BOTH normal builds and publishes -->
  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <!-- manual version you want to show as v1.0 -->
      <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>

      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
      <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\obj\$(Configuration)\$(TargetFramework)\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Ensure the file exists -->
    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <!-- If BuildNumber wasn't set by Publish, just read the current counter -->
    <ReadLinesFromFile Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''" File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''">
      <BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = "$(ProductVersion)"
    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = "$(BuildNumber)"

    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String
        Get
            Return "AMiOffice v" & ProductVersion & " (Build " & BuildNumber & ")"
        End Get
    End Property
End Module
" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <!-- Avoid duplicates, then include generated file -->
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>
```

## 3) Set your window title / About text
Use:

```vbnet
Me.Text = BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

(or `"Active Magnetic Inspection - " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`)

## How it behaves
- Click **Build**: build number stays the same (reads `build-counter.txt`)
- Click **Publish**: build number increments by 1, and the published app shows the new number

If you got “Unexpected end of file… Target, Project line 118”, it almost certainly means the file ends before one of those closing tags.

```xml
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
```

Your file is actually **well‑formed XML** (all `Target` and `Project` tags are closed) — so the “unexpected end of file… Target, Project line 118” error is almost certainly coming from **a truncated/partial file on disk** or a **hidden character/encoding issue** after you pasted.

That said, there are two practical fixes that eliminate the common causes:

1) ensure there’s a newline at end of file and nothing after `</Project>`
2) avoid multi-line MSBuild task attributes that sometimes get mangled by copy/paste (especially if the editor inserts smart quotes or invalid characters)

Below is a **sanitized, paste-safe** replacement `AMiOffice.vbproj` where I:
- keep all your content,
- keep build-counter publish increment,
- keep BuildInfo generation,
- but reformat the XML to be less fragile (no indentation/tab oddities, and the big `WriteLinesToFile` is still one attribute but the XML is otherwise clean).

You’ve got **duplicate source files being compiled** (likely the “Recovered” project is including the same files twice, or you have two generated files / AssemblyInfo files). That’s why you see:

### Fix: remove the duplicate compile items (most common: you now have two BuildInfo files + an extra AssemblyInfo file)

Do these steps in this order.

## 1) Fix the BuildInfo module conflict
You currently have **two BuildInfo modules** being compiled. Common causes:
- a manually-created `BuildInfo.vb` or `BuildInfo.g.vb` in the project folder
- plus the generated one in `obj\...\BuildInfo.g.vb`

### What to do
In Solution Explorer, search for files named:
- `BuildInfo.vb`
- `BuildInfo.g.vb`
- anything similar in the project root

If you find a real file in the project (not under `obj\`), **exclude it from project** or rename it.

### In the `.vbproj`, add this to ensure you never compile any stray BuildInfo generated files outside obj
Put this near the bottom (before `</Project>`):

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <!-- Ensure we don't compile a copied/generated BuildInfo from anywhere else -->
  <Compile Remove="**\BuildInfo.g.vb" />
  <Compile Remove="**\BuildInfo.vb" Condition="Exists('BuildInfo.vb')" />
</ItemGroup>
```

(We’ll re-include the one we generate via `$(BuildInfoFile)` as you already do.)

## 2) Fix “Attribute … cannot be applied multiple times” (AssemblyInfo duplication)
This happens when **both** are true:
- SDK project auto-generates assembly info (`<GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>`)
- AND you also have an `AssemblyInfo.vb` (or similar) in `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb` with attributes.

**Option B: keep the old AssemblyInfo.vb, disable auto-generation**
Add / set in `<PropertyGroup>`:

```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
```

Most WinForms VB projects that came from older templates have `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb`, so Option B is often quickest.

That part is normal: you’ll always have **two generated files** (one per configuration) under `obj\Debug\...` and `obj\Release\...`. Those **do not** conflict with each other because you only build one configuration at a time.

So the `module 'BuildInfo' ... conflict` error means you have **another `BuildInfo` module being compiled from somewhere else**, most commonly:

- the generated file is being included **twice** in the same build (once via your explicit `<Compile Include="...BuildInfo.g.vb" />`, and once via the SDK’s default glob include), or
- you have a second copy of the project’s source files being globbed in (causing *everything* to be duplicated), which would also explain the `Public Sub New()` duplicates and the `TargetFrameworkAttribute` duplicates.

### Fix 1 (very likely): stop explicitly including the generated file (let SDK default include it)
Right now you have both:
- default SDK behavior: includes `**\*.vb` (including anything under `obj` **if it gets picked up**, depending on settings)
- plus your explicit `<Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" ... />`

The safest approach: generate the file into `obj` but **do not explicitly include it**; instead generate it into a known “Generated” folder *outside* `obj`, or explicitly exclude `obj` from Compile includes and include only the generated file once.

#### Easiest reliable fix: generate into `Generated\BuildInfo.g.vb` (not obj)
1) Create folder: `Generated` under your project.
2) Change this property:

```xml
<BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\Generated\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>
```

3) In your target, keep:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
</ItemGroup>
```

This guarantees it’s included exactly once and avoids obj weirdness.

### Fix 2 (also required): AssemblyInfo duplication
Your errors `TargetFrameworkAttribute` and `AssemblyCompanyAttribute` etc. “cannot be applied multiple times” almost always means you have an `AssemblyInfo.vb` file **and** `<GenerateAssemblyInfo>true</GenerateAssemblyInfo>`.

#### Do this now
In the `.vbproj` set:

```xml
<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
```

(Unless you want to delete `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb`, but disabling generation is quickest.)

### Fix 3: the `MyApplication` / `Sub New()` duplicates
Those happen when the same designer/codebehind is compiled twice. If after Fix 1 + Fix 2 you *still* get those, it means the project is including source files from two locations.

Common cause: in a recovered copy, the `.vbproj` lives in a folder that contains *another copy* of the project as a subfolder, and the SDK default glob pulls both in.

Example bad layout:
- `AMiOffice\`  
  - `Form1.vb`
  - `AMiOffice\`  ← second nested copy also containing `Form1.vb`

## Likely root cause in your `.vbproj`: these `<Import Include=...>` lines
You currently have:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Import Include="System.Data" />
  <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
  <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
</ItemGroup>
```

In SDK-style VB projects, global imports are usually controlled by `ImplicitUsings` / defaults; manually forcing these sometimes triggers weird behavior, and given your crash is in `ParseImports`, I’d remove them.

### Fix: remove the whole Import ItemGroup
Delete this entire block from the `.vbproj`:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Import Include="System.Data" />
  <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
  <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
</ItemGroup>
```

Then clean `bin/obj` again and rebuild.

## If it STILL crashes: find the bad VB file (fast binary search)
A compiler crash is often a single file with a bad character. Here’s the fastest way:

1) In Solution Explorer, **temporarily unload/exclude** half your `.vb` files (start with `My Project\*.Designer.vb`, `*.Designer.vb`, `*.resx`-paired files).

**Most common culprits:**
- `My Project\Application.Designer.vb`
- `My Project\Resources.Designer.vb`
- any `Form*.Designer.vb`

When you find it, open it in Notepad++ and check:
- Encoding (save as UTF-8)
- Any weird characters at end of lines
- Truncated file / missing `End Class` / etc.

## Fix: tell MSBuild to only compile files under the project directory

```xml
<EnableDefaultItems>false</EnableDefaultItems>
```

Then explicitly include your project’s VB files.

### Easiest explicit include (everything under the project folder, excluding bin/obj)
Add this **ItemGroup**:

```xml
<ItemGroup>

  <EmbeddedResource Include="**\*.resx" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <None Include="**\*" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**;**\*.vb;**\*.resx" />
</ItemGroup>
```

That forces the scope to the project folder only, and will stop pulling in:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project\...`
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\obj\...`

```xml
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo

        Get

        End Get
    End Property
End Module
" />

    <ItemGroup>

      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
```

### Fastest guaranteed fix (no MSBuild spelunking)
Rename or delete the stray folders at the repo root so they can’t be picked up:

1) Close Visual Studio
2) In File Explorer:
- Rename `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\My Project` → `My Project - stray` (or delete it)
- Rename `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\obj` → `obj - stray` (or delete it)

3) Reopen VS → Rebuild

Those two folders **should not exist at repo root** (only under your project). Removing them will immediately eliminate:
- the duplicate `Application.Designer.vb` compile
- the duplicate generated assembly attribute files

### The “proper” MSBuild fix (if you want it robust even if those folders exist)
Add these two properties to your `<PropertyGroup>` (in addition to what you already have):

```xml
<BaseIntermediateOutputPath>obj\</BaseIntermediateOutputPath>
<IntermediateOutputPath>obj\$(Configuration)\</IntermediateOutputPath>
```

And add a hard exclusion that also catches items introduced later:

```xml
<Target Name="RemoveRogueRepoRootCompiles" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="..\My Project\**\*.vb" />
    <Compile Remove="..\obj\**\*.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Target>
```

Why a Target? Because if `Directory.Build.targets` (higher up) adds `Compile Include=...` after your ItemGroups, this will still remove them right before compile.

Do the rename/delete first (2 minutes). It’s the cleanest because those folders are wrong to have there anyway.

These errors mean your forms are no longer being compiled as **WinForms types** (so `Label`, `Panel`, `Timer`, and events like `SizeChanged`/`Tick` aren’t in scope). This happens when you disabled the SDK’s default items and *didn’t bring back the VB global imports / WinForms references the way VB expects*.

### Fix: turn VB implicit/global imports back on the *right* way (not via `<Import Include=...>`)
In SDK-style **VB** projects, WinForms designer code typically relies on default VB imports (like `System.Windows.Forms`) being available globally.

Do this in your **inner** `AMiOffice.vbproj`:

## 1) Remove the explicit `EnableDefaultItems/EnableDefaultCompileItems` workaround (now that you’re using the correct project)
Set these back to default by deleting them (or setting them to true):

- Remove:
```xml
<EnableDefaultItems>false</EnableDefaultItems>
<EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>
```

Also remove the manual “include everything” ItemGroup:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Include="**\*.vb" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <EmbeddedResource Include="**\*.resx" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**" />
  <None Include="**\*" Exclude="bin\**;obj\**;**\*.vb;**\*.resx" />
</ItemGroup>
```

Because the SDK already does this correctly and also sets up the right behavior for WinForms designer.

## 2) Ensure these WinForms properties exist (you already have them, but confirm)
```xml
<UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
<MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>
```

## 3) Add VB’s implicit imports explicitly (safe + fixes your current errors)
Add this to your main `<PropertyGroup>`:

```xml
<VbMyType>WindowsForms</VbMyType>
<ImplicitUsings>true</ImplicitUsings>
```

(If `VbMyType` isn’t recognized in your SDK version, it won’t hurt; `MyType` is the main one.)

## 4) If you still need protection from the repo-root stray folders
Instead of disabling default items, keep default items ON and just exclude those rogue paths explicitly:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Remove="..\My Project\**\*.vb" />
  <EmbeddedResource Remove="..\My Project\**\*" />
  <None Remove="..\My Project\**\*" />

  <Compile Remove="..\obj\**\*.vb" />
  <EmbeddedResource Remove="..\obj\**\*" />
  <None Remove="..\obj\**\*" />
</ItemGroup>
```

But if you deleted/renamed those stray folders already, you can skip this.

You’re still opening a **solution filter** (`AMiOffice.slnx`). That’s the root of the “projects getting mixed / blank / missing projects” behavior.

A `.slnx` is *not* the authoritative solution; it just points at a solution and selects a subset of projects. If its target solution or project paths changed (you renamed the root `.vbproj` to `.bak`, moved folders, etc.), the `.slnx` can end up empty or pointing at the wrong project.

Those 17 errors mean the compiler is treating your forms as if **Windows Forms isn’t referenced at all**. When WinForms is correctly enabled, `Label`, `Panel`, `Timer`, `SizeChanged`, `Tick`, etc. are all known.

So we need to confirm/fix **one of these** (in order of likelihood):

1) The project is not actually targeting **Windows** (or WinForms isn’t enabled in the *effective* project properties)
2) `UseWindowsForms` is not being applied (wrong `<PropertyGroup>` condition, overridden by `Directory.Build.props`, etc.)
3) Your designer files lost their `Inherits System.Windows.Forms.Form` or the project’s **RootNamespace** / imports are broken

Your `.vbproj` is missing the *single most important thing* for VB WinForms here: it’s **not using the VB SDK**. You currently have:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
```

For **VB** Windows Forms projects, this should be:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk.WindowsDesktop">
```

Without the WindowsDesktop SDK, you can still sometimes compile, but the WinForms surface area and references (and designer expectations) often won’t be wired up correctly—leading exactly to `Label` / `Panel` / `Timer` “not defined”.

## Fix: change the SDK line
Change the first line to:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk.WindowsDesktop">
```

Nothing else needs to change to prove the point.



### After you rebuild
If any errors remain, paste them—at that point we’ll be dealing with real code issues, not missing WinForms types.
Error NETSDK1137 It is no longer necessary to use the Microsoft.NET.Sdk.WindowsDesktop SDK. Consider changing the Sdk attribute of the root Project element to 'Microsoft.NET.Sdk'.

Got it—on **.NET 10 / VS 2026**, WinForms is supported with plain `Microsoft.NET.Sdk`, and the `WindowsDesktop` SDK is deprecated, hence `NETSDK1137`. So revert that SDK line back to `Microsoft.NET.Sdk`.

Your WinForms types still not resolving means: **the WinForms reference is not being added at build time**, despite `<UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>`. The most common reason in a “recovered” repo is a higher-level MSBuild file overriding things (e.g., `Directory.Build.props/targets`) or the project is being evaluated with a non-Windows target somehow.

Let’s do two deterministic fixes:

## 1) Add an explicit framework reference to Windows Forms (this is the real missing piece)
In your `.vbproj`, keep:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
```

Add this **ItemGroup** (near the top, after the main PropertyGroup is fine):

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <FrameworkReference Include="Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.WindowsForms" />
</ItemGroup>
```

This forces the WinForms framework reference even if something is overriding the normal behavior of `UseWindowsForms`.

## 2) Add explicit Imports for designer compilation (belt-and-suspenders)
Also add:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Import Include="System" />
  <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
  <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
</ItemGroup>
```

(These are VB “global imports” controlled by MSBuild; they’re different from `Imports` statements in code.)

> You previously mentioned a “blank page issue” tied to Imports; that crash was likely from the earlier duplicate-compilation mess. With the solution now clean, these global imports are safe and commonly used to stabilize recovered VB projects.

## Here is your “completely fixed” `.vbproj` (full file)
Replace your current file with this:

```xml name=AMiOffice.vbproj
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0-windows</TargetFramework>
    <StartupObject>AMiOffice.My.MyApplication</StartupObject>

    <UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>
    <MyType>WindowsForms</MyType>

    <AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>
    <FileVersion>1.0.0.0</FileVersion>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>

    <GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>
    <InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>

    <BuildInfoFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\Generated\BuildInfo.g.vb</BuildInfoFile>

    <Copyright>Copyright Kirk Saffell 2026</Copyright>
    <Title>AMiOfficeApplication</Title>
    <ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Force WinForms framework reference (fixes 'Label/Panel/Timer not defined') -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <FrameworkReference Include="Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.WindowsForms" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- VB global imports to make designer code compile reliably -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Import Include="System" />
    <Import Include="System.Windows.Forms" />
    <Import Include="System.Drawing" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Hard-stop: never compile stray repo-root folders -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="..\My Project\**\*.vb" />
    <Compile Remove="..\obj\**\*.vb" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Remove="ESC Logo.jpg" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Include="ESC Logo.jpg">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>Never</CopyToOutputDirectory>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Update="My Project\Application.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Application.myapp</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Resources.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTime>True</DesignTime>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Resources.resx</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>

    <Compile Update="My Project\Settings.Designer.vb">
      <DesignTimeSharedInput>True</DesignTimeSharedInput>
      <AutoGen>True</AutoGen>
      <DependentUpon>Settings.settings</DependentUpon>
    </Compile>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <EmbeddedResource Update="My Project\Resources.resx">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My.Resources</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>VbMyResourcesResXFileCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Resources.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </EmbeddedResource>
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <None Update="My Project\Application.myapp">
      <Generator>MyApplicationCodeGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Application.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>

    <None Update="My Project\Settings.settings">
      <CustomToolNamespace>My</CustomToolNamespace>
      <Generator>SettingsSingleFileGenerator</Generator>
      <LastGenOutput>Settings.Designer.vb</LastGenOutput>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- Prevent compiling any stray BuildInfo file from the project root -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="BuildInfo.g.vb" />
    <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="$(BuildNumber)" />
  </Target>

  <Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildCounterFile>$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt</BuildCounterFile>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <WriteLinesToFile
      Condition="!Exists('$(BuildCounterFile)')"
      File="$(BuildCounterFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="0" />

    <ReadLinesFromFile Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''" File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(BuildNumber)' == ''">
      <BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <MakeDir Directories="$([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName('$(BuildInfoFile)'))" />

    <WriteLinesToFile
      File="$(BuildInfoFile)"
      Overwrite="true"
      Lines="Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Friend Module BuildInfo
    Friend Const ProductVersion As String = "$(ProductVersion)"
    Friend Const BuildNumber As String = "$(BuildNumber)"

    Friend ReadOnly Property DisplayVersion As String
        Get
            Return "AMiOffice v" & ProductVersion & " (Build " & BuildNumber & ")"
        End Get
    End Property
End Module
" />

    <ItemGroup>
      <Compile Remove="$(BuildInfoFile)" />
      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>
```

## Fix the error: `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp` doesn’t exist
Your generated `BuildInfo.g.vb` module currently defines only:

- `ProductVersion`
- `BuildNumber`
- `DisplayVersion`

…but your code in `FormMainMenu.vb` line 16 references `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp`.

### Option A (recommended): add `BuildTimestamp` to the generated BuildInfo module
Edit the `GenerateBuildInfo` target in your `.vbproj` where it writes `Lines="...` and add one more constant, e.g.:

```vb
Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = "2026-04-20T12:34:56Z"
```

Because this is generated at build time, use MSBuild’s `System.DateTime`:

**Change your `WriteLinesToFile` “Lines” string to include:**
- `Friend Const BuildTimestamp As String = "$(BuildTimestamp)"`

…and define `BuildTimestamp` in the target:

Add this inside `<Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo" ...>` before `WriteLinesToFile`:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildTimestamp>$([System.DateTime]::UtcNow.ToString("yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ"))</BuildTimestamp>
</PropertyGroup>
```

That will satisfy `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp`.

### Option B: remove the reference from code
In `FormMainMenu.vb` line 16, change it to use `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion` (which you already generate) and stop referencing `BuildTimestamp`.

## About the WinForms framework warning (NETSDK1086)
You can remove this now:

- `FrameworkReference Include="Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.WindowsForms"`

Because with `TargetFramework=net10.0-windows` and `<UseWindowsForms>true</UseWindowsForms>`, the SDK already adds it.

So: **delete that FrameworkReference ItemGroup** to silence NETSDK1086.

## About all the CA1416 warnings
These are analyzers complaining “reachable on all platforms”. But your project is **Windows-targeted** (`net10.0-windows`). Two easy ways:

### If you just want them gone (recommended for WinForms apps)
Add to your main `<PropertyGroup>`:

```xml
<EnableNETAnalyzers>false</EnableNETAnalyzers>
```

Or, more targeted:

```xml
<NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CA1416</NoWarn>
```

(Only do this if you’re comfortable ignoring platform-compat warnings.)

2) Remove the explicit WinForms `FrameworkReference` (optional, to silence warning).

weren't we using buildtimestamp to add a build number that appeared as a date or time stamp previously and I asked to remove that?

Yes. Earlier you had a timestamp-style build identifier, and you asked to remove it in favor of the **publish-only build counter** + `DisplayVersion`. What’s happening now is simply: **the code still references the old field** (`BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp`) even though the generator no longer emits it.

So the correct fix is to **remove the usage** of `BuildTimestamp` (don’t bring it back).

## Do this fix (recommended)

Open `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\FormMainMenu.vb` and at/around **line 16** replace whatever uses `BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp` with `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion` (or just `BuildInfo.BuildNumber` if you only want the number).

Examples:

### If you were building a window title like “AMiOffice …”
Change from something like:
```vb
Me.Text = "AMiOffice " & BuildInfo.BuildTimestamp
```

to:
```vb
Me.Text = BuildInfo.DisplayVersion
```

(or)
```vb
Me.Text = $"AMiOffice v{BuildInfo.ProductVersion} (Build {BuildInfo.BuildNumber})"
```

That will clear the **only error**.

both the main menu window title and the about box now display AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 0)

That means your `BuildInfo.g.vb` is being generated, but `build-counter.txt` is still `0` (or you’re always reading `0`). Since you changed it so the counter only increments on **Publish**, normal builds will keep showing whatever number is currently in `build-counter.txt`.

## Fix it (choose what you want)

### Option A (recommended): keep “increment only on Publish”, but set the initial number
Open (or create) this file:

`C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\build-counter.txt`

Put a number in it, e.g.:

```
1
```

Save, rebuild. Now the UI will show Build 1, and it will only change when you Publish.

### Option B: verify Publish actually increments it
Do a publish once:
- **Build → Publish AMiOffice**
Then check that `build-counter.txt` changed (e.g., to 2).

If it did not change, your `IncrementBuildCounter` target isn’t firing (usually because you’re using a publish method that doesn’t run the `Publish` target you think it does).

### Option C: increment on every build (not just Publish)
Change:
```xml
<Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
```
to:
```xml
<Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="CoreCompile">
```

That will bump the build number every time you build/debug.

## Quick confirmation question
Open `build-counter.txt` and tell me what it currently contains (exact contents). If it’s `0`, Option A will immediately fix your display. If it’s not `0`, then `GenerateBuildInfo` is reading the wrong file or the property isn’t being set correctly and I’ll walk you through that next.
also, how can I save this entire Visual Basic Project so that all files, .vbproj, .slnx etc. all get saved and backed up so I can have an external version to fall back on if something happens again?

To “save everything” for a Visual Basic project, you don’t rely on Visual Studio’s Save (that only saves edited files). You want a **full-folder backup** plus (ideally) **version control**.

## Best practice (recommended): put the project under Git and push it somewhere
This gives you unlimited rollback and protects you from exactly what happened.

1) In Visual Studio: **Git → Create Git Repository**
2) Choose **Local only** first (or GitHub if you want)
3) Add a `.gitignore` (VS can generate one)
4) Commit.
5) Push to GitHub (private repo is fine) or another remote.

**Important:** do *not* commit build outputs:
- `bin/`
- `obj/`
- `.vs/`

But **do** commit:
- `AMiOffice.vbproj`
- `AMiOffice.slnx`
- all `*.vb`, `*.resx`, images, `build-counter.txt`, etc.

If you want, tell me whether you want GitHub or just local Git and I’ll give the exact click-by-click steps in VS 2026.

## Simple “external backup” (no Git): copy the right folders/files
1) Close Visual Studio (prevents locked files and partial writes).
2) Copy the **project root folder**:
   - `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\`
   to an external drive or a backup folder like:
   - `E:\Backups\AMiOffice\AMiOffice_backup_2026-04-20\`

3) Before copying (or after), delete these to reduce size and avoid stale build junk:
- `C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\.vs` (if present)
- `**\bin`
- `**\obj`

This backup method is dumb-but-reliable: if something breaks, you restore the folder.

## “One file” backup: zip it
After closing VS and deleting `bin/obj/.vs`:
- Right-click the `AMiOffice` folder → **Send to → Compressed (zipped) folder**
- Store the zip on an external drive/cloud.

## What NOT to back up
Avoid relying on:
- only `.slnx`
- only `.vbproj`
because the app also needs all the forms/resources/images/settings files.

Change your command to pass an explicit publish directory.

Notes:

- If you don’t want the `^` line continuations, you can put it all on one line:

```bat
dotnet publish "C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj" -c Release /p:PublishProfile=Folder-win-x64 /p:PublishDir="C:\Users\CapnKirk\Downloads\AM Conversion\VisStudBuilds\Build 14 after recovered from 46 errors\"
```

If your publish profile is also setting an output folder, `PublishDir` on the command line will override it.
C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\AMiOffice.vbproj(114,7): error MSB4184: The expression "[System.Int32]::Parse('1') + 1" cannot be evaluated.

That error is coming from this line in your `.vbproj`:

```xml
<BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
```

In MSBuild property functions you must use the **fully-qualified** `System.Int32`, not `[System.Int32]`.

## Fix
Change it to:

```xml
<BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
```

Wait—yours already *looks* like that, but your error message shows:

```
"[System.Int32]::Parse('1') + 1"
```

So somewhere it is actually using `[System.Int32]` (square brackets), likely:

```xml
<BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
```

No—MSBuild uses `$([Type]::Method())` syntax; the *problem* is your expression is missing the `$()` wrapper around the addition, or you’re doing `Parse('1') + 1` outside the property function evaluation.

### The safest working form
Replace your increment block with this (this always evaluates correctly):

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)'))</BuildNumber>
  <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Add($(BuildNumber), 1))</BuildNumber>
</PropertyGroup>
```

But MSBuild doesn’t have `Int32.Add` as a static method in that way; better:

**Use one expression with fully qualified type:**
```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
</PropertyGroup>
```

This *should* work. Since you’re getting MSB4184, the actual issue is usually that `%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)` is being treated as an item and the quoting is collapsing weirdly, or the line is not inside a `$()` properly in your real file.

## Deterministic fix: use MSBuild’s `Add` property function instead of `+`
MSBuild supports arithmetic via property functions using `System.Int32` but `+` can be finicky in some contexts. Do this:

Replace the increment with:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)'))</BuildNumber>
  <BuildNumber>$([MSBuild]::Add($(BuildNumber), 1))</BuildNumber>
</PropertyGroup>
```

That avoids `Parse(...) + 1` entirely.

## What to change (exactly where)
In your `IncrementBuildCounter` target, replace:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)') + 1)</BuildNumber>
</PropertyGroup>
```

with:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <BuildNumber>$([System.Int32]::Parse('%(BuildCounterLines.Identity)'))</BuildNumber>
  <BuildNumber>$([MSBuild]::Add($(BuildNumber), 1))</BuildNumber>
</PropertyGroup>
```

Then publish again.

You have two separate things going on in the About dialog:

1) the fields it’s showing (“(not Set)”, “My”, 0.0.0.0) are coming from the **assembly metadata** / **Application Framework** info, not from your `BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`.
2) your assembly metadata is currently not being populated the way VB’s built-in About dialog expects, because you set `<GenerateAssemblyInfo>false</GenerateAssemblyInfo>` and you likely don’t have a proper `My Project\AssemblyInfo.vb` providing the attributes.

### Why it says **“My”** at the top
That’s the VB “Application Framework” name/title coming from the `My` application info (often `My.Application.Info.Title` / `AssemblyTitle`). When it can’t find a proper title/product, it falls back to odd defaults like “My”.

### Why it says **(not Set)** and **0.0.0.0**
Those are classic symptoms of missing assembly attributes such as:
- `AssemblyTitle`
- `AssemblyProduct`
- `AssemblyCompany`
- `AssemblyVersion` / `AssemblyFileVersion`

Right now, you set `<AssemblyVersion>1.0.0.0</AssemblyVersion>` etc. in the project, but because `GenerateAssemblyInfo` is off, those may **not** get emitted into the assembly unless you also provide attributes in `AssemblyInfo.vb`.

Use this as a complete replacement for `FormAbout.vb`. It forces the title to **AMiOffice**, shows your **BuildInfo.DisplayVersion**, and pulls **AssemblyVersion / FileVersion / ProductVersion** from the built executable so you won’t get `(not Set)` or `0.0.0.0` once assembly info generation is enabled.

Also: this assumes you have labels named (at least) `lblTitle`, `lblDisplayVersion`, `lblAssemblyVersion`. For the rest, I’ve used common names—if yours differ, either rename your labels to match or tell me your actual label names and I’ll adjust.

```vbnet name=FormAbout.vb
Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Imports System.Diagnostics
Imports System.Reflection
Imports System.Windows.Forms

Public Class FormAbout

    Private Sub FormAbout_Load(sender As Object, e As EventArgs) Handles MyBase.Load
        ' Always show a stable app title (avoid "My")
        lblTitle.Text = "AMiOffice"

        ' Your custom build display (e.g. "AMiOffice v1.0 (Build 14)")
        lblDisplayVersion.Text = SafeText(BuildInfo.DisplayVersion, "AMiOffice")

        ' Assembly version (from VB Application Framework)
        lblAssemblyVersion.Text = My.Application.Info.Version.ToString()

        ' File/Product version come from the built EXE metadata
        Dim exePath As String = Application.ExecutablePath
        Dim fvi As FileVersionInfo = FileVersionInfo.GetVersionInfo(exePath)

        ' If you have these labels, populate them; otherwise remove these lines.
        If ControlExists("lblFileVersion") Then
            DirectCast(Controls.Find("lblFileVersion", True)(0), Label).Text =
                SafeText(fvi.FileVersion, My.Application.Info.Version.ToString())
        End If

        If ControlExists("lblProductVersion") Then
            DirectCast(Controls.Find("lblProductVersion", True)(0), Label).Text =
                SafeText(fvi.ProductVersion, BuildInfo.ProductVersion)
        End If

        If ControlExists("lblProductName") Then
            DirectCast(Controls.Find("lblProductName", True)(0), Label).Text =
                SafeText(fvi.ProductName, "AMiOffice")
        End If

        If ControlExists("lblCompanyName") Then
            DirectCast(Controls.Find("lblCompanyName", True)(0), Label).Text =
                SafeText(fvi.CompanyName, "")
        End If

        If ControlExists("lblCopyright") Then
            DirectCast(Controls.Find("lblCopyright", True)(0), Label).Text =
                SafeText(fvi.LegalCopyright, "")
        End If
    End Sub

    Private Shared Function SafeText(value As String, fallback As String) As String
        If String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(value) Then
            Return fallback
        End If
        Return value
    End Function

    Private Function ControlExists(controlName As String) As Boolean
        Dim matches = Me.Controls.Find(controlName, True)
        Return matches IsNot Nothing AndAlso matches.Length > 0
    End Function

End Class
```

Replace your entire `FormAbout.vb` with the following. It uses your actual control names (`lblAppName`, `rtbInfo`, etc.), shows the same build display as the main menu (`BuildInfo.DisplayVersion`), and fills in assembly/file/product versions from the built EXE metadata. It also avoids ever showing “(not Set)”.

```vbnet name=FormAbout.vb
Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Imports System.Diagnostics
Imports System.Reflection
Imports System.Text

Public Class FormAbout

    Private Sub FormAbout_Load(sender As Object, e As EventArgs) Handles MyBase.Load
        ' Title bar text
        Me.Text = "About AMiOffice"

        ' Big red app name line (your designer label)
        lblAppName.Text = "AMiOffice"

        ' Optional: make the "About:" header include the build display
        lblAbout.Text = "About: " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion

        ' Show detailed info in the rich text box
        rtbInfo.Clear()
        rtbInfo.SelectionAlignment = HorizontalAlignment.Left
        rtbInfo.Font = New Font("Consolas", 9.75F, FontStyle.Regular)

        Dim exePath As String = Application.ExecutablePath
        Dim fvi As FileVersionInfo = FileVersionInfo.GetVersionInfo(exePath)

        Dim sb As New StringBuilder()

        sb.AppendLine(BuildInfo.DisplayVersion)
        sb.AppendLine()

        sb.AppendLine($"Assembly Version : {My.Application.Info.Version}")
        sb.AppendLine($"File Version     : {Safe(fvi.FileVersion)}")
        sb.AppendLine($"Product Version  : {Safe(fvi.ProductVersion)}")
        sb.AppendLine()

        sb.AppendLine($"Product Name     : {Safe(fvi.ProductName, "AMiOffice")}")
        sb.AppendLine($"Company          : {Safe(fvi.CompanyName)}")
        sb.AppendLine($"Copyright        : {Safe(fvi.LegalCopyright)}")
        sb.AppendLine()

        sb.AppendLine($"Executable Path  : {exePath}")

        rtbInfo.Text = sb.ToString()

        ' If you want a nicer "Developed By" line, set it here:
        ' (Change the name to whatever you want displayed.)
        lblDevelopedBy.Text = "Developed By: Kirk Saffell"

        ' If you want to show an embedded image in picDeveloper, do it here.
        ' (Only if you have one available. Otherwise leave it blank.)
        ' picDeveloper.Image = My.Resources.YourImageName
    End Sub

    Private Shared Function Safe(value As String, Optional fallback As String = "") As String
        If String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(value) Then
            If String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(fallback) Then
                Return "(not set)"
            End If
            Return fallback
        End If
        Return value.Trim()
    End Function

End Class
```

      <Compile Include="$(BuildInfoFile)" Visible="false" />
    </ItemGroup>
  </Target>

</Project>

You’re extremely close—your `.vbproj` just has **one big metadata bug**: you define `<InformationalVersion>` **twice**, and the second one overwrites the first. Right now you have:

- `InformationalVersion = $(ProductVersion) (Build $(BuildNumber))`  ✅ (what you want)
- later: `InformationalVersion = $(Version)` ❌ (overrides it)

So your built EXE’s product/info version won’t include the build number, and depending on what your About form reads, you’ll see inconsistent results like “(not set)”.

## 1) Fix the duplicate InformationalVersion
Delete this line entirely:

```xml
<InformationalVersion>$(Version)</InformationalVersion>
```

Keep only this one (near the top is fine):

```xml
<InformationalVersion>$(ProductVersion) (Build $(BuildNumber))</InformationalVersion>
```

## 2) Add a deterministic fallback BuildNumber for normal builds
Right now `BuildNumber` is often empty unless Publish ran (or the read happens). To ensure your assembly metadata always has a number even on a normal build, add this to your main `<PropertyGroup>`:

```xml
<BuildNumber Condition="'$(BuildNumber)'==''">0</BuildNumber>
```

Then your informational version is always well-formed.

## 3) Optional: make ProductVersion match your assembly Version
You currently have:
- `<ProductVersion>1.0</ProductVersion>`
- `<Version>1.0.0.0</Version>`

That’s fine, but if you want consistency, you can do:

```xml
<ProductVersion>$(Version)</ProductVersion>
```

(or set `Version` to `1.0.0` and keep `ProductVersion` as `1.0`—your call).

You currently have **two different `FormAbout` classes** being compiled, so the Designer partial class can’t “see” the controls and even thinks it’s not a Form anymore. That’s why you’re getting:

- `class 'FormAbout' and class 'FormAbout' conflict`
- Designer says `Event 'Load' cannot be found`
- Designer says `Text is not a member of 'FormAbout'`
- controls like `lblAppName` “not declared”

This happens when **FormAbout.vb is not declared as a Partial class**, or there is **another FormAbout class in another file/namespace**.

## Fix (do exactly this)

### 1) Make `FormAbout.vb` a `Partial Class FormAbout`
Replace your entire `FormAbout.vb` with this version (note the **Partial** keyword):

```vbnet name=FormAbout.vb
Option Strict On
Option Explicit On

Imports System.Diagnostics
Imports System.Text

Partial Public Class FormAbout

    Private Sub FormAbout_Load(sender As Object, e As EventArgs) Handles MyBase.Load
        ' Title bar text
        Me.Text = "About AMiOffice"

        ' Big red app name line (your designer label)
        lblAppName.Text = "AMiOffice"

        ' "About:" header include build display
        lblAbout.Text = "About: " & BuildInfo.DisplayVersion

        ' Developed by line
        lblDevelopedBy.Text = "Developed By: Kirk Saffell"

        ' Fill the info box
        Dim exePath As String = Application.ExecutablePath
        Dim fvi As FileVersionInfo = FileVersionInfo.GetVersionInfo(exePath)

        Dim sb As New StringBuilder()
        sb.AppendLine(BuildInfo.DisplayVersion)
        sb.AppendLine()
        sb.AppendLine($"Assembly Version : {My.Application.Info.Version}")
        sb.AppendLine($"File Version     : {Safe(fvi.FileVersion)}")
        sb.AppendLine($"Product Version  : {Safe(fvi.ProductVersion)}")
        sb.AppendLine()
        sb.AppendLine($"Product Name     : {Safe(fvi.ProductName, "AMiOffice")}")
        sb.AppendLine($"Company          : {Safe(fvi.CompanyName)}")
        sb.AppendLine($"Copyright        : {Safe(fvi.LegalCopyright)}")
        sb.AppendLine()
        sb.AppendLine($"Executable Path  : {exePath}")

        rtbInfo.Text = sb.ToString()
    End Sub

    Private Shared Function Safe(value As String, Optional fallback As String = "") As String
        If String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(value) Then
            If String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(fallback) Then
                Return "(not set)"
            End If
            Return fallback
        End If
        Return value.Trim()
    End Function

End Class
```

**Key detail:** `Partial Public Class FormAbout` must match the designer’s `Partial Class FormAbout`.

```
Class FormAbout
```

If you find another file defining `Class FormAbout` (not the Designer file), it must be deleted/renamed, or moved into a different namespace.

### 3) Clean rebuild
Close VS, delete `bin` and `obj` under `...\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\`, reopen, rebuild.

When the partial class linkage breaks (because your code-behind wasn’t `Partial`, or there’s a second class with the same name), the designer half is no longer inheriting from `System.Windows.Forms.Form` as a single combined type, so VB complains that Form events/properties don’t exist.

1) make sure there is exactly **one** `FormAbout` partial class definition in non-designer code
2) ensure the file is compiled **once** and the designer file is paired correctly

## Even simpler: fix recognition by adding DependentUpon entries
If you’d rather keep the existing files, we can reattach them in the `.vbproj` by explicitly declaring the designer relationships.

Add this to your `.vbproj`:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <Compile Update="FormAbout.Designer.vb">
    <DependentUpon>FormAbout.vb</DependentUpon>
  </Compile>
  <EmbeddedResource Update="FormAbout.resx">
    <DependentUpon>FormAbout.vb</DependentUpon>
  </EmbeddedResource>
</ItemGroup>
```

This often brings “View Designer” back *after* the designer file is clean.

You didn’t break anything “mysteriously” — two specific regressions happened:

1) **Build number went back to 0** because your `IncrementBuildCounter` target only runs on the MSBuild `Publish` target, and **your batch file `dotnet publish ... /p:PublishProfile=...` is very likely not invoking the exact MSBuild target you think** (or it is, but the build counter file being read is `0`). The Learn docs even note the `Publish` target is ClickOnce-oriented in some contexts. ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/visualstudio/msbuild/msbuild-targets?view=visualstudio&utm_source=openai))  
2) **About dialog lost the picture + share paths** because the code-behind you restored into the designer file didn’t include any of the logic that was previously setting `picDeveloper.Image` and writing the share paths into the rich text box. In other words: we restored layout + version text, but not your old “content” logic.

## 1) Fix BuildNumber showing 0 (make it reliable for your batch publish)

### A. Make sure `build-counter.txt` lives where you think
Your project reads:

- `$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)\build-counter.txt`

So it must be here:
`C:\Users\CapnKirk\source\repos\AMiOffice\AMiOffice\build-counter.txt`

Open it and confirm it contains (for example):
```
14
```
(no extra blank lines).

### B. Don’t rely on `BeforeTargets="Publish"` for filesystem publish
In SDK-style projects, depending on how publish is invoked, `Publish` is not always the hook you expect. ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/visualstudio/msbuild/msbuild-targets?view=visualstudio&utm_source=openai))  
Instead, hook into a publish pipeline target that **always** runs when you do `dotnet publish`.

Replace your counter target header:

```xml
<Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish">
```

with:

```xml
<Target Name="IncrementBuildCounter" BeforeTargets="Publish;PublishOnly;ComputeFilesToPublish">
```

That makes it fire for more publish paths.

### C. Add a message so you can see it running
Inside the target add:

```xml
<Message Text="IncrementBuildCounter running. BuildCounterFile=$(BuildCounterFile)" Importance="high" />
<Message Text="New BuildNumber=$(BuildNumber)" Importance="high" />
```

Then run your batch file and you should see those lines.

If you want, paste the console output around those messages and I’ll confirm it’s running.

```vbnet name=FormAbout.vb
    Private Sub FormAbout_Load(sender As Object, e As EventArgs) Handles MyBase.Load
        ' Window / header labels

        Dim fvi As FileVersionInfo = FileVersionInfo.GetVersionInfo(exePath)

        Dim sb As New StringBuilder()

        ' App identity
        sb.AppendLine("AMiOffice")
        sb.AppendLine($"Version: {My.Application.Info.Version}")
        sb.AppendLine($"File Version: {Safe(fvi.FileVersion)}")
        sb.AppendLine($"Build: {BuildInfo.DisplayVersion}")
        sb.AppendLine()

        ' Author/company info (from your screenshot)
        sb.AppendLine("Author/Developer: Kirk Saffell")
        sb.AppendLine("Company: Enterprise Services & Consulting")
        sb.AppendLine("Phone: (661) 478-0990")
        sb.AppendLine("Contact: capnkirk@capnkirk.com")
        sb.AppendLine("Year: 2026")
        sb.AppendLine()

        ' Machine info
        sb.AppendLine($"Machine: {Environment.MachineName}")
        sb.AppendLine($"User: {Environment.UserName}")
        sb.AppendLine($"OS: {RuntimeInformation.OSDescription}")
        sb.AppendLine($".NET Runtime: {RuntimeInformation.FrameworkDescription}")
        sb.AppendLine()

        ' Shared paths (adjust these strings if needed)
        sb.AppendLine("Shared paths:")

        Dim dataPath As String = "\\invoice\MainMenu\Data"
        Dim backupsPath As String = "\\invoice\MainMenu\Backups"
        Dim wordPath As String = "\\invoice\word"

        sb.AppendLine($"Data: {dataPath} {ExistsTag(dataPath)}")
        sb.AppendLine($"Backups: {backupsPath} {ExistsTag(backupsPath)}")
        sb.AppendLine($"Word: {wordPath} {ExistsTag(wordPath)}")

        rtbInfo.Text = sb.ToString()
    End Sub

    Private Shared Function ExistsTag(path As String) As String
        Try
            If Directory.Exists(path) Then
                Return ""
            End If
        Catch
            ' ignore (permissions/network issues) and treat as not found
        End Try

        Return "[NOT FOUND]"
    End Function

    Private Shared Function Safe(value As String, Optional fallback As String = "") As String
        If String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(value) Then
            If String.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(fallback) Then Return "(not set)"
            Return fallback
        End If
        Return value.Trim()
    End Function

    Private Shared Function LoadEmbeddedImage(candidateNames As IEnumerable(Of String)) As Image
        Dim asm As Assembly = Assembly.GetExecutingAssembly()
        Dim allNames As String() = asm.GetManifestResourceNames()

        For Each candidate In candidateNames
            ' Try exact match first
            Dim exact As String = allNames.FirstOrDefault(Function(n) String.Equals(n, candidate, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase))
            If exact Is Nothing Then
                ' Try "ends with" match (common with VB root namespace prefixes)
                exact = allNames.FirstOrDefault(Function(n) n.EndsWith(candidate, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase))
            End If

            If exact IsNot Nothing Then
                Using s As Stream = asm.GetManifestResourceStream(exact)
                    If s IsNot Nothing Then
                        Return Image.FromStream(s)
                    End If
                End Using
            End If
        Next

        Return Nothing
    End Function

End Class
```

at the top, or add `<Import Include="System.Linq" />` in the vbproj. (Your vbproj currently doesn’t import Linq.)

If `build-counter.txt` is **15** and you still see **Build 0**, then your build is not reading the same file (or it’s not getting the value into `BuildInfo.g.vb` at compile time).

Here’s the quickest, deterministic way to fix it.

## 1) Ensure MSBuild reads the counter line as a *property* (not an item expression)
Right now in `GenerateBuildInfo` you do:

```xml
<BuildNumber>%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)</BuildNumber>
```

That `%(...)` item metadata syntax is fragile inside a `PropertyGroup` and is the #1 reason people end up with blank/0 values.

### Replace your `GenerateBuildInfo` read section with this
In your `.vbproj`, inside `Target Name="GenerateBuildInfo"`, replace everything from the `<ReadLinesFromFile ...>` through the conditional `<PropertyGroup ...>` with this block:

```xml
    <ReadLinesFromFile File="$(BuildCounterFile)">
      <Output TaskParameter="Lines" ItemName="BuildCounterLines2" />
    </ReadLinesFromFile>

    <!-- Convert first line item into a real property -->
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.String]::Copy('%(BuildCounterLines2.Identity)'))</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>

    <!-- Trim + fallback -->
    <PropertyGroup>
      <BuildNumber>$([System.String]::Copy('$(BuildNumber)').Trim())</BuildNumber>
      <BuildNumber Condition="'$(BuildNumber)'==''">0</BuildNumber>
    </PropertyGroup>
```

That forces the first line into a normal MSBuild property and trims whitespace.

## 2) Add a high-importance message so you can SEE what it read
Still inside `GenerateBuildInfo` (after the property group above), add:

```xml
    <Message Text="GenerateBuildInfo: BuildCounterFile=$(BuildCounterFile) BuildNumber=$(BuildNumber)" Importance="high" />
```

Now when you build, the Output window / build log will literally show the number MSBuild is using.

yes, on the workstation everything appears fine except the shared data paths do not saying either OK or Not found

That’s just the `ExistsTag()` logic: right now it returns **blank when the folder exists**, and only prints `[NOT FOUND]` when it doesn’t. On your workstation the shares likely exist, so you’re seeing nothing.

Change it so it explicitly shows `[OK]` when the path exists.

## Replace `ExistsTag` with this
In `FormAbout.vb`, replace your current function:

```vbnet
Private Shared Function ExistsTag(path As String) As String
    Try
        If Directory.Exists(path) Then Return ""
    Catch
    End Try
    Return "[NOT FOUND]"
End Function
```

with:

```vbnet
Private Shared Function ExistsTag(path As String) As String
    Try
        If Directory.Exists(path) Then Return "[OK]"
    Catch
        ' network/permission issues => treat as not found
    End Try
    Return "[NOT FOUND]"
End Function
```

Now your About box will show:

- `\\invoice\MainMenu\Data  [OK]` (when reachable)
- `... [NOT FOUND]` (when not)

all .bas files are from DOS 5.0 GWBasic.exe

No—we’re not ready to “bring in ledger.bas” directly as source code, because **DOS GW-BASIC `.BAS` is not compatible with VB.NET** (or even VB6) and cannot be compiled/imported into your WinForms project as-is. We *can* bring the functionality in, but it’s a **port/rewrite** effort.

### What this means in practice
GW-BASIC programs typically rely on:
- line numbers (`10 PRINT ...`)
- `GOTO/GOSUB/RETURN`
- `ON ERROR GOTO`
- `DEF FN`
- `DATA/READ/RESTORE`
- `INPUT`, `INKEY$`, screen/keyboard control, and direct console UI
- fixed-length record files / random access (`OPEN "R"`, `FIELD`, `PUT/GET`)
- sometimes `CHAIN`, `COMMON`, etc.

None of that maps 1:1 to WinForms.

---

## Best path forward (recommended)
### Phase 1: Decide your “ground truth”
Pick one:
1) **Keep GW-BASIC as the reference** and port module-by-module to VB.NET, validating outputs match.
2) **Treat the data as the reference** (ledgers/files) and re-implement the business rules in VB.NET, validating against known reports.

### Phase 2: Get it running in a controlled way
For `ledger.bas`, we start by extracting:
- Inputs (files, parameters, user prompts)
- Outputs (reports, files, totals)
- Data formats (record layouts)

Then we build a VB.NET “ledger engine” library (plain classes/modules) and call it from your WinForms UI.

Start with **LEDGER** (the one invoked by `CHAIN "LEDGER"` / menu option C). It’s the fastest/easiest to implement first because it’s a “business-logic + file I/O” program you can port into a **single VB.NET workflow** without having to recreate the entire DOS UI/keyboard behavior.

### Why LEDGER is the best first file (based on your `MAINMENU.BAS` snippet)
Your main menu does a lot of DOS-only things that are **hard** to port directly:

- `DEF SEG=&H40 : POKE/PEEK` (keyboard LEDs / caps/num lock)
- `KEY` macro programming
- `INKEY$` loops + screen blanker
- `LOCATE`, `COLOR`, ASCII art UI
- heavy `SHELL`, `CHAIN`, `SYSTEM`, floppy/drive assumptions (`A:\`, `F:\`, `D:\`)
- lots of “operator experience” features (beeps, time-based logic)

Those are “polish” features and **not** where you want to start.

By contrast, the “real work” programs behind menu items (like `LEDGER`, `SALES`, `LOGBOOK`, `QUOTE`, `PHONE/ROLODEX`) usually have:
- defined inputs/outputs
- persistent files
- deterministic calculations
- report printing

Those are much more straightforward to port and test.

---

## What I need from you to begin porting LEDGER
To port `LEDGER.BAS` into VB.NET, I need just two things from you first:

1) **Convert `LEDGER.BAS` to ASCII and paste/upload it** (or upload the file).
2) Also paste/upload any **data file specs** it touches (even if you don’t know the format yet).  
   I’ll discover them from `OPEN` statements, but sample files help.

### When you paste it, include at least:
- the top of the file
- every `OPEN` / `FIELD` / `GET` / `PUT` / `INPUT #` / `WRITE #` line
- any `TYPE`-like record layout comments (sometimes they’re in comments)

---

## After LEDGER, the next easiest set
Once LEDGER is in, good next targets (usually) are:

1) `SALES` (Sales Journal) — often similar file/report patterns
2) `LOGBOOK` (view/add) — typically append-only text/data
3) `QUOTE` (form generator) — might be print formatting heavy but doable
4) `PLIST` (price list) — can be more UI-heavy depending on editing/search

I’d postpone anything that is mostly DOS shell/utility work, like:
- “backup/restore” routines (lots of path assumptions and `SHELL ZIP/UNZIP/XCOPY`)
- keyboard cleaning mode / typewriter mode / screen saver
- “send a message” batch-file generation
- anything that launches external DOS apps

### What “ASCII” means here
You want the file to be human-readable in Notepad and look like:

- line numbers (`10`, `20`, `30`…)
- keywords like `OPEN`, `INPUT#`, `GOSUB`, etc.
- quoted strings visible

- Open the program in GW-BASIC/QBASIC/QuickBASIC

**Option B (if you only have the tokenized file):**

I'm having trouble converting ledger.bas. I'm loading gwbasic, the loading ledger.bas, then F4 for save, saving the file like this: SAVE"LEDGER.BAS,A and it's not saving as text

In GW-BASIC the ASCII flag is **not** part of the filename string. It’s a second argument to `SAVE`.

Use this exact syntax (note the comma is **outside** the quotes):

```basic
SAVE "LEDGER.ASC", A
```

or, if you want to keep the `.BAS` extension:

```basic
SAVE "LEDGER.BAS", A
```

What you typed:

```basic
SAVE"LEDGER.BAS,A
```

puts `,A` *inside* the filename (and also has quoting issues), so it won’t do an ASCII save.

## Step-by-step (GW-BASIC)
1) Run `GWBASIC`
2) `LOAD "LEDGER.BAS"`
3) At the `Ok` prompt, type:

```basic
SAVE "LEDGER.ASC", A
```

4) You should see `Ok` again.
5) Then `SYSTEM` (or exit) and check the output file in the same directory GW-BASIC is using.

## How to confirm it worked
Open `LEDGER.ASC` in Notepad:
- you should see line numbers like `10`, `20`, `30`…
- readable keywords (`PRINT`, `OPEN`, `GOSUB`, etc.)

## Common gotcha: wrong working directory
If you don’t see the file where you expect, inside GW-BASIC do:

```basic
FILES
```

That will show the current directory contents so you can confirm where it saved.

That output is exactly what a **tokenized GW-BASIC program** looks like when it’s opened/printed as raw bytes (mojibake). It means the “save as ASCII” did **not** happen, or you’re viewing the wrong file after saving.

Here’s how to fix it reliably.

## 1) Use the correct GW-BASIC command (don’t use the F4 dialog)
In GW-BASIC, do this at the `Ok` prompt:

```basic
LOAD "LEDGER.BAS"
SAVE "LEDGER.TXT", A
```

Key points:
- The comma and `A` are **outside** the quotes.
- Use a *new* filename like `LEDGER.TXT` so you don’t accidentally re-open the original tokenized file.

Then:

```basic
SYSTEM
```

## 2) Verify you’re looking at the ASCII file, not the original
Back in DOS (or File Explorer), make sure you open **LEDGER.TXT** in Notepad, not `LEDGER.BAS`.

Inside GW-BASIC you can confirm the file exists with:

```basic
FILES "LEDGER.*"
```

You should see both `LEDGER.BAS` and `LEDGER.TXT`.

## 3) If `SAVE "LEDGER.TXT", A` gives an error
Tell me the exact message. Common ones:

### A) “Bad file name”
You’re in the wrong directory or using a path GW-BASIC can’t write to.
Fix: `CHDIR` to a writable folder, or use a simple name with no path.

### B) “Direct statement in file”
The program contains something GW-BASIC can’t store in ASCII format (rare, but it happens).
Fix: We can still extract it using `LIST` to a file (next section).

## 4) Fallback method that always works: redirect LIST to a file
If for any reason `SAVE ..., A` won’t work, do:

```basic
LOAD "LEDGER.BAS"
FILES
LIST
```

(Confirm it displays readable code on screen. If it does, it can be captured.)

Then do:

```basic
OPEN "LEDGER.TXT" FOR OUTPUT AS #1
PRINT #1, " "   ' (optional, just to create/truncate)
CLOSE #1
```

Unfortunately GW-BASIC doesn’t have an easy built-in “LIST to file” like some BASICs, but there is a common workaround using `LLIST` (printer) if your environment supports redirecting LPT1 to a file:

```basic
LOAD "LEDGER.BAS"
LLIST
```

And in DOS you can redirect printer output to a file if LPT1 is captured. Whether this works depends on DOSBox/Windows setup.

So: **try `SAVE "LEDGER.TXT", A` first**—it’s the correct and simplest approach.

- Real DOS
- DOSBox
- Windows NTVDM
- Something else

```basic
LOAD "LEDGER.BAS"
LIST 1-20
```

From `LEDGER.BAS`, the fastest/easiest feature to port into your VB.NET app is:

**“View Checks”** (menu option **3**, starts at line **460**), because it:
